abstract: Within the theological landscape of Afrikaans-speaking South Africa, Rudolf Bultmann’s work—particularly his program of demythologization—was historically met with significant skepticism and little positive regard. More than seventy years have passed since his seminal 1941 essay ignited a global debate, and for many contemporary scholars, Bultmann’s legacy has largely faded into the periphery of academic discourse. However, the 130th anniversary of his birth provides a timely opportunity to revisit this influential text. This paper seeks to engage directly with Bultmann’s original voice, setting aside decades of secondary interpretations to offer a fresh perspective. Now that the dust has settled on the controversies of the mid-twentieth century, a re-examination may reveal that Bultmann’s insights remain surprisingly relevant for our current theological context.
